title: Wire androidTest coverage into JaCoCo aggregator so Compose / instrumentation-tested code counts
description:
## Why
JaCoCo currently reports overall coverage in the ~28% range despite ViewModels being unit-tested at 95–100% (e.g. LoginViewModel: 0 missed / 130 covered, FeedViewModel: 97.5%). The number is dominated by uncovered Compose code that unit tests can't reach but instrumentation / screenshot tests do:

| Package | Missed | What's there |
|---|---:|---|
| `:designsystem/component` | 6887 (36% of all missed) | PostCardKt 1827, PostCardQuotedPostKt 752, etc. |
| `:feature:feed:impl` | 2300 | FeedScreenKt: 1785 missed / 0 covered |
| `:feature:feed:impl/ui` | 2140 | FeedAppendingIndicator, FeedEmptyState, ... |
| `:designsystem` | 1902 | NubecitaTheme, modifiers |
| `:designsystem/preview` | 1540 | @Preview composables |
| `:feature:login:impl` | 921 | LoginScreenKt: 915 missed / 0 covered |

~70% of all missed instructions are Compose-related code that IS tested via screenshot or instrumentation tests — but JaCoCo only ingests `testDebugUnitTest.exec` files today. The new instrumentation tests landed under epic nubecita-9tw (Phase 1: nubecita-mqi feed, nubecita-6fd login, future nubecita-z9d auth) contribute zero to the jacoco number under the current wiring.

## What to wire

In `build-logic/convention/src/main/kotlin/.../AndroidJacocoConventionPlugin.kt`:

1. **Pull androidTest .ec files into the aggregator's `executionData`**:
   ```kotlin
   internal fun Project.jacocoExecutionData(): ConfigurableFileCollection =
       files(
           fileTree(layout.buildDirectory) {
               include(
                   "jacoco/testDebugUnitTest.exec",
                   "outputs/unit_test_code_coverage/debugUnitTest/testDebugUnitTest.exec",
                   // NEW: connectedAndroidTest emits a separate .ec per device.
                   "outputs/code_coverage/debugAndroidTest/connected/**/*.ec",
               )
           },
       )
   ```
2. **Enable androidTest coverage at the variant level** (the AGP knob). Likely lives in the AndroidLibraryConventionPlugin / AndroidApplicationConventionPlugin, on the debug buildType:
   ```kotlin
   buildTypes.getByName("debug").apply {
       enableUnitTestCoverage = true
       enableAndroidTestCoverage = true   // NEW
   }
   ```
3. **Decide the report task wiring.** Options:
   - (A) Keep jacocoTestReport unit-only; add a sibling `jacocoAndroidTestReport` and `jacocoAggregatedReport` that merges both. The aggregated report is what CI's coverage comment pulls.
   - (B) Have jacocoTestReport depend on connectedDebugAndroidTest. Pros: one number on every PR. Cons: forces the (~5–6 min) instrumented job onto every coverage run; affects merge-gate latency.
   Recommend (A) — keep unit-test coverage as the fast feedback loop, surface androidTest coverage as a separate metric or only in the merged number on push-to-main.
4. **Update `jacocoClassExcludes`** to also drop Hilt androidTest generated artifacts so they don't show up in the denominator.

## Verification
- `./gradlew :feature:feed:impl:connectedDebugAndroidTest jacocoTestReportAggregated` produces a report where FeedScreenKt is no longer 0% covered.
- The CI `Test` job's coverage comment shows a meaningfully higher overall % once the instrumented job runs alongside.
- Existing unit-test coverage doesn't drop (sanity check: don't accidentally exclude class directories that were previously included).

## Out of scope
- Switching to Kover, JaCoCo XL, or any tooling change.
- Adding new tests; this is wiring only.

## Effort
~2–3 hours. The risk is in (3) — the report-task wiring needs to handle the case where connectedDebugAndroidTest hasn't run (local dev) without breaking jacocoTestReportAggregated; either guard the .ec inclusion or skip-when-empty.

title: Investigate why Create AVD snapshot step runs on cache hit (potential ~1:27 CI win)
description:
## Symptom
The `instrumented` job in `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` consistently spends ~1m 27s in the `Create AVD snapshot` step even though the preceding `AVD cache` step (using `actions/cache@v5` with key `avd-cache-v1-api35-pixel6`) completes in ~0s and the trailing `Post AVD cache` step is `skipped` (= cache wasn't re-saved because it already existed at the same key). The create step has `if: steps.avd-cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'` and should be skipped when the cache hits.

Sample data (6 recent runs of the instrumented job, all on `main` push):

| Run | Total | Create AVD snapshot | Run instrumented tests |
|-----|------:|--------------------:|----------------------:|
| 25201223583 | 5:17 | ~1:27 | ~2:30 |
| 25204718559 | 5:23 | 1:27 | 2:33 |
| 25226226579 | 5:28 | ~1:27 | ~2:30 |
| 25149193571 | 5:29 | ~1:27 | ~2:30 |
| 25222613197 | 5:35 | ~1:27 | ~2:35 |
| 25223981590 | 8:20 (outlier) | (presumed cache miss) | ~5:00 |

## Hypothesis
`steps.avd-cache.outputs.cache-hit` is reporting `false` even when the AVD path is restored. Possible causes:
- GitHub Actions cache scoping by branch — main creates the cache, feature branches see it via fallback only with `restore-keys`, currently not configured. But these runs are on `main` itself, so this shouldn't apply.
- The cached path `~/.android/avd/*` doesn't include the AVD definition file outside the directory (e.g., `~/.android/avd/<name>.ini` lives at the parent level), so the action's restore is partial → `cache-hit` flips to false.
- Some quirk of `actions/cache@v5` post-step behavior we're misreading.

## Diagnostic spike (~30 min)
1. Add a temp step BEFORE `Create AVD snapshot`:
   ```yaml
   - name: Debug AVD cache state
     run: |
       echo "cache-hit: '${{ steps.avd-cache.outputs.cache-hit }}'"
       ls -la ~/.android/avd/ || echo 'no avd dir'
       ls -la ~/.android/avd/*/ || echo 'no avd contents'
   ```
2. Push to a branch with `run-instrumented` label, observe the output.
3. Decide remediation:
   - If `cache-hit: false` but path is populated → expand cache key path to include `~/.android/avd/*.ini` (the AVD index), re-test.
   - If `cache-hit: true` and step still ran → investigate `if:` evaluation; possibly the conditional is malformed in some edge case.
   - If `cache-hit: true` and step is skipped now (cache state changed since last run) → close as not-reproducible.

## Win
Successful skip of the create step on cache hit drops the instrumented job from ~5:19 → ~3:50 (~28% faster). Material on PRs labeled `run-instrumented` and on every push to `main`.

## Out of scope
- Gradle Managed Devices migration (research flagged GMD as still flaky on GitHub-hosted runners through 2025).
- Test parallelization / sharding (premature at 5 tests; revisit when nearing 20).
- Switching to Firebase Test Lab (cost/benefit not there yet).

## Verification
After remediation: run the workflow on a feature branch with `run-instrumented` label; total instrumented job duration should be ≤ 4:00 with the create step shown as `skipped` in the steps list. Re-run the same branch a second time to confirm the optimization is stable across sequential cache-hit runs (not flaky).

title: Flip CI instrumented job from opt-in label to required-on-PR
description:
Once the three Phase 1 tests have been green on CI for ~3 consecutive PRs without retry, flip .github/workflows/ci.yaml so the 'instrumented' job runs on every PR (no 'run-instrumented' label gate) and is a required check.

## Scope
- Edit .github/workflows/ci.yaml: remove the label-conditional from the 'instrumented' job's 'if'; keep the job timeout at 45 min; AVD cache stays.
- Update branch-protection rules in GitHub if maintained as code (likely a manual step in repo settings).
- README / CONTRIBUTING update if instrumented tests are documented.

## Soak gate
Do NOT land this until the three Phase 1 tests are green for 3 consecutive non-retried PR runs.

## Verification
A subsequent unrelated PR runs the instrumented job automatically and goes green; check shows up in GitHub UI as required.

Refs: nubecita-9tw

title: chore(deps): bump atproto-kotlin 5.1.3 → 5.2.0 + adopt upstream AtUri.parse()
description:
Follow-up to nubecita-8f6.1 (Like/repost data layer). The implementation includes an inline `parseAtUri` helper and a `TODO(atproto-kotlin#57)` marking where to replace it once the upstream library ships an `AtUri.parse()` helper. atproto-kotlin 5.2.0 is now released on Maven Central with that helper landed, so the cleanup is unblocked.

## Scope

### Version bump

- `gradle/libs.versions.toml:3`: `atproto = "5.1.3"` → `"5.2.0"`. Single key powers all five artifact aliases:
  - `atproto-compose`
  - `atproto-compose-material3`
  - `atproto-models`
  - `atproto-oauth`
  - `atproto-runtime`

### Inline-parser removal

- Delete the `parseAtUri` helper added in nubecita-8f6.1 (location lands once that PR merges — likely in the like/repost repository or a shared helper file).
- Replace call sites with the upstream `AtUri.parse()` (or whatever the 5.2.0 release names it — confirm via [atproto-kotlin#57](https://github.com/kikin81/atproto-kotlin/issues/57) once the bump goes through).
- Preserve the record-shape assertions from the Copilot review on 8f6.1 (must have `collection` and `rkey`, reject/strip fragment) — those remain valuable post-bump and just wrap the upstream `AtUriParts` result instead of string-splitting.
- Drop the `TODO(atproto-kotlin#57)` comment.

## Why a separate PR (not bundled into 8f6.1)

- **Blast radius is the whole app, not one module.** Re-linking touches `:core:auth`, `:designsystem`, `:feature:feed:impl`, `:data:models`, `:app` — every module that imports atproto types. Bundling a 5-module classpath change into a feature PR muddies review.
- **Triage isolation.** If something regresses post-merge, "feature broke" and "library broke" are easier to bisect when they're separate commits.
- **Tight, easily-reviewable diff.** Bump + helper swap = one focused PR with its own bd id and audit trail (helps future `git blame` on the version line).

## Acceptance

- `gradle/libs.versions.toml` shows `atproto = "5.2.0"`.
- No remaining call sites of the inline `parseAtUri` helper anywhere under `./*`.
- No remaining `TODO(atproto-kotlin#57)` comments.
- `./gradlew :app:assembleDebug spotlessCheck lint testDebugUnitTest` clean.
- `./gradlew jacocoTestReportAggregated` doesn't regress on touched modules.

## Out of scope

- Other dep bumps (Renovate handles those independently).
- Restructuring how AT-URI parsing is exposed (utility location, multi-module sharing) — only swap the impl one-for-one.
close_reason: Obsolete — atproto-kotlin bump to 5.2.6 shipped via Renovate PR #92 (skipped 5.2.0 entirely), and the upstream AtUri.parseOrNull() adoption that was the other half of this task shipped via PR #93 (DefaultLikeRepostRepository now delegates to AtUri.parseOrNull). No further work needed. Branch was never used for commits.

title: test(feature/feed): instrumented coverage for PostShareLauncher.launchPostShare
description:
Follow-up to nubecita-8f6.4 (PR #91). The share feature was deliberately split into:

- `PostShareIntent` + `PostUi.toShareIntent()` — pure Kotlin builder, 100% unit-tested in `PostShareIntentTest`.
- `PostShareLauncherKt.launchPostShare` — thin Android `Context` extension that fires `ACTION_SEND` + `Intent.createChooser`. **0% coverage by design** — needs an instrumentation harness to exercise.

This ticket adds that harness so the share package hits 100% line coverage end-to-end, not just on the unit-testable seam.

## Scope

Pick one of:

- **Robolectric unit test** (preferred — runs on `testDebug` JVM source set, no emulator needed). Use `Shadows.shadowOf(application).nextStartedActivity` to capture the dispatched intent and assert on `action == ACTION_SEND`, `type == "text/plain"`, `EXTRA_TEXT == intent.permalink`, and that the wrapper is a chooser (`ACTION_CHOOSER` + `EXTRA_INTENT` matches the inner send).
- **Instrumented test on `androidTest`** — only if Robolectric proves awkward for chooser introspection.

Robolectric path likely lands in ~30 lines. The `compile.dependencies.androidTest` already pulls in the test runner from the project's instrumented-test stack (PR #75); Robolectric would need a small dep add to the existing JVM test classpath.

## Acceptance

- A test in `feature/feed/impl/src/test/kotlin/.../share/PostShareLauncherTest.kt` (or analogous instrumented path) that fires `launchPostShare` and asserts the dispatched intent shape.
- `./gradlew :feature:feed:impl:jacocoTestReport` shows `PostShareLauncherKt` ≥ 95% line coverage.
- `./gradlew jacocoTestReportAggregated` doesn't regress on any other module.

## Out of scope

- Compose UI call sites (FeedScreen, PostCard, PostCallbacks) — project-wide pattern is to cover those via screenshot tests, not unit tests. Tracked as a separate concern if/when we adopt Compose UI testing more broadly.

## Why

PR #91's split was intentional for testability of the builder. The launcher is "glue we trust" today, but covered glue beats uncovered glue — and Robolectric makes it cheap.

title: chore(skills): bd-worktree drops PreToolUse bd-blocking hook into new worktrees
description:
The bd-worktree skill documents that bd writes from inside a worktree go to an isolated dolt server and are silently lost on worktree removal. The 'Don'ts' section warns Claude not to invoke bd from within a worktree, but interactive Claude sessions reflexively call `bd show`/`bd update` and instructions alone aren't enforced.

Harden the skill so each new worktree gets a PreToolUse hook that hard-blocks any Bash command matching `(^|[\\s;&|()])bd([\\s]|$)`. The hook + settings are written into the new worktree's `.claude/` directory at create time and are git-ignored via the existing `.claude/*` pattern.

Tested against the two existing worktrees (`nubecita-5l3`, `nubecita-6wa`): blocks `bd ready`, `cd /tmp && bd close foo`; allows `git status`, `ls /tmp/bd-data` (no false positive on `bd-` substring).

## Acceptance

- `.claude/skills/bd-worktree/SKILL.md` documents the hook + settings to drop and updates the bd-isolation gotcha + Don'ts sections to point to the hook as enforcement.
- Future `bd worktree create` invocations following the skill drop the hook into the new worktree.
close_reason: Merged via PR #83

title: chore(skills): add bd-worktree skill for sibling-path worktree workflow
description:
Codify the worktree-creation workflow as a project-local skill at .claude/skills/bd-worktree/SKILL.md. Companion to the existing bd-workflow skill: handles the worktree-specific path/claim/cleanup ceremony.

Captures hard-won lessons from the nubecita-8m4 epic:
- Worktrees must live OUTSIDE the repo (../nubecita-<bd-id>) — spotless and gradle walk the filesystem ignoring .gitignore, so any worktree under the repo root breaks the build.
- bd worktree create spawns an isolated dolt server in each worktree, so bd update/bd close issued from inside the worktree land in the wrong DB. Pre-claim from main before creating the worktree, or skip the claim entirely.
- Path convention is ../nubecita-<bd-id> (terse + stable) rather than feature-name-based which drifts.

Trigger phrases: 'kick off a worktree for nubecita-X', 'start worktree for X', 'create worktree for X'.
close_reason: Merged via PR #79 (commit 171e1be)

title: ci(workflows): tighten instrumented-tests cadence — cron + release + path-filter
description:
## Why

The instrumented-tests CI job (added in nubecita-16a) currently runs on every push to main, every workflow_dispatch, and on PRs gated by the `run-instrumented` label. Running on every push to main is overkill — chore / refactor / CI / docs / openspec PRs trigger ~10-15 min of runner time for tests that don't exercise the changed code.

This ticket tightens the cadence to a more sustainable pattern (NIA / Tivi style) without losing regression-catching power: path-filter the push-to-main trigger, add a daily cron, and add the release event as a final pre-publish gate.

## Scope

Edit `.github/workflows/ci.yaml`:

1. **Path-filter the existing `push: branches: [main]` trigger** so the workflow (specifically the instrumented job) only fires when commits touch source paths that could plausibly break instrumented tests:

   ```yaml
   push:
     branches: [main]
     paths:
       - 'app/**'
       - 'core/**'
       - 'feature/**'
       - 'designsystem/**'
       - 'data/**'
       - 'gradle/**'
       - 'build-logic/**'
   ```

   Note: this affects ALL jobs in ci.yaml. If we want push-to-main path-filtering to apply only to the instrumented job (not lint/test/build/screenshot), the cleanest path is to split instrumented out into a separate workflow file. Decide during implementation — most repos with similar setups just path-filter the whole CI workflow on push because the other jobs are also pure overhead on docs-only changes.

2. **Add a daily cron trigger** for the workflow. Bounds the bisect window for instrumented regressions at ~1 day of merges.

   ```yaml
   schedule:
     - cron: '0 7 * * *'  # daily 07:00 UTC, before US/EU work hours
   ```

   And update the `instrumented` job's `if:` condition to include `github.event_name == 'schedule'`.

3. **Add the release event** to the workflow triggers so the instrumented job is the final gate before semantic-release publishes:

   ```yaml
   release:
     types: [created]
   ```

   And update the `instrumented` job's `if:` condition to include `github.event_name == 'release'`.

## Acceptance

- `ci.yaml` triggers include push (path-filtered), pull_request, workflow_dispatch, schedule, and release.
- Instrumented job's `if:` condition gates execution on label-presence (PR), or any of: workflow_dispatch / push / schedule / release.
- Pushing a docs-only PR merge (no source changes under filtered paths) does NOT fire instrumented.
- Pushing a source PR merge DOES fire instrumented.
- Manual workflow_dispatch fires instrumented.
- A scheduled run fires instrumented at 07:00 UTC daily.

## Bisect runbook (document inline in this ticket's notes after implementation)

When nightly cron goes red:
1. Find the last green nightly via `gh run list --workflow=ci.yaml --event=schedule --limit=10`.
2. List merges since: `git log --oneline <last-green-sha>..main`.
3. Re-run instrumented on each suspect merge: `gh workflow run ci.yaml --ref <commit-sha>` (the workflow_dispatch trigger respects the ref input).
4. First red SHA is the regression. Open a fix-up PR.

## Non-goals

- Splitting instrumented into its own workflow file. Possible follow-up if the path-filter on the unified CI workflow proves too coarse (e.g., we want `Lint` to still run on docs-only changes but want `Instrumented` skipped). Defer until we observe the real pattern in practice.
- Caching the emulator AVD across cron runs (already cached via actions/cache@v4 keyed by api-level + profile; bumps invalidate cleanly).

title: feat(app): App Check enforce mode + stable CI debug keystore
description:
## Why

Following nubecita-e7x, App Check is wired in **monitor mode** — provider factories are installed but rejected tokens still flow. The threat model the original ticket cited (fork-driven analytics pollution from anyone reusing the public-repo's `google-services.json`) is only mitigated once enforcement is enabled. After ~3 days of monitor-mode telemetry confirms legitimate traffic carries valid tokens, flip enforcement to enforce.

When enforcement turns on, **CI-distributed debug APKs start failing token validation** unless we control the signing keystore. The GitHub Actions runner auto-generates its own `debug.keystore` per run with a SHA-1 we can't pre-register in Firebase. Solution: generate a single shared `nubecita-debug.keystore`, store it as a GitHub secret, have CI sign with it, and register its SHA-256 fingerprint in Firebase.

This ticket bundles both concerns — flipping enforce without stable CI signing would break debug distribution to internal testers.

## Scope

### Stable CI debug keystore
- Generate `nubecita-debug.keystore` locally (one-shot; NOT committed). Document the `keytool -genkey` command and password handling in the ticket runbook.
- Compute SHA-1 + SHA-256 fingerprints; register both under Firebase project `nubecita-2a4c1` → app `net.kikin.nubecita` → SHA certificate fingerprints.
- Encode keystore as base64. Add GitHub repository secrets: `DEBUG_KEYSTORE_BASE64`, `DEBUG_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`, `DEBUG_KEY_ALIAS`, `DEBUG_KEY_PASSWORD`.
- In `release.yaml` `distribute` job: decode the keystore to a runner-local path before `assembleDebug`. Configure `:app/build.gradle.kts` `signingConfigs.debug` to read keystore + credentials from env-provided values when present, falling back to the developer's `~/.android/debug.keystore` otherwise.
- Verify CI-built debug APKs sign with the registered fingerprint (`apksigner verify --print-certs build/outputs/apk/debug/app-debug.apk`).

### App Check enforce flip
- After ≥3 days of monitor-mode telemetry shows real traffic carries valid tokens with no spurious rejections, flip App Check enforcement from **Monitor** → **Enforce** in Firebase console for Analytics (and any other Firebase services in use).
- Verify: stripping App Check init from a local build → events stop appearing in Firebase Analytics. Restoring init → events resume.

## Acceptance

- `nubecita-debug.keystore` SHA-1 + SHA-256 are registered in Firebase console.
- GitHub secrets `DEBUG_KEYSTORE_BASE64`, `DEBUG_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`, `DEBUG_KEY_ALIAS`, `DEBUG_KEY_PASSWORD` are set.
- CI `distribute` job decodes the keystore and signs `assembleDebug` with it.
- `apksigner verify` on a CI-distributed APK matches the registered fingerprint.
- App Check enforcement is flipped to **Enforce** in Firebase console.
- Internal testers continue receiving App Distribution emails.
- App continues sending Analytics events post-enforcement (verified in DebugView and aggregate dashboards).

## Non-goals

- Production release signing — separate ticket (Play App Signing).
- Per-developer debug-keystore unification. Developers continue using their own `~/.android/debug.keystore`; per-device App Check debug tokens cover that path under enforcement.
- Crashlytics, Firebase Performance Monitoring, Firebase Remote Config — separate tickets per nubecita-e7x non-goals.

## References

- Originating ticket: nubecita-e7x — Firebase integration.
- App Check Play Integrity (Android): https://firebase.google.com/docs/app-check/android/play-integrity-provider
- App Check enforcement modes: https://firebase.google.com/docs/app-check/monitor-metrics

title: perf(designsystem): collapse Compose layers in PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed
description:
## Background

`PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed` (shipped under `nubecita-umn`) intentionally composes two sibling leaf composables — a media embed (Images / Video / External) on top + `PostCardQuotedPost` below, separated by an 8 dp Spacer inside a `Column`. Each leaf creates its own Compose / GPU layer.

## Observed regression (umn 95th percentile vs 6vq baseline)

Captured via `dumpsys gfxinfo` over a recordWithMedia-weighted Pixel 10 Pro XL release-build scroll session (`-PdebugSignedRelease=true` + `-PcomposeReports=true`):

| Metric | 6vq baseline | umn (PR #64) | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Janky frames | 0.22% | 0.46% | +0.24pp |
| 95th percentile frame | 7ms | 10ms | +3ms (over the 8.33 ms 120 Hz budget) |
| 99th percentile frame | 12ms | 13ms | +1ms |
| GPU 99th percentile | 2ms | 8ms | +6ms |
| Slow issue draw commands | 1 | 8 | +7 |

Histogram delta: the 8-12 ms bucket grew from 3.9% (287 / 7,323) on 6vq to 8.0% (499 / 6,266) on umn. Steady-state scroll (50th/90th percentile) is unchanged — the regression is on the over-budget tail, concentrated where `recordWithMedia` posts enter / leave the visible band.

## Hypothesis

Each `PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed` instance stacks two leaf composables vertically:

1. Media leaf — `PostCardImageEmbed` / `PostCardExternalEmbed` / `PostCardVideoEmbed` (host-supplied)
2. `PostCardQuotedPost` — already a `Surface(surfaceContainerLow)` per 6vq

Both leaves likely allocate their own RenderNode at the GPU layer, doubling the per-item draw work for recordWithMedia rows compared to a plain quote (one Surface) or a plain media embed (one leaf). The +6ms GPU 99th-percentile + 8x "Slow issue draw commands" supports this.

## Investigation directions

- Profile via Layout Inspector + Perfetto on a recordWithMedia-heavy fixture feed. Confirm the layer count vs 6vq.
- Try `Modifier.graphicsLayer` hoisting at the `PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed` root (single offscreen layer instead of two siblings).
- Try restructuring the layout: `Column` → `Layout` with a single RenderNode + custom drawing? Likely overkill but worth measuring.
- Investigate whether the Video case is the hottest sub-shape (player surface + poster + quoted card = three layers).
- Compare against the official Bluesky Android client's `gfxinfo` for the same shape to set a realistic ceiling.

## Acceptance

- 95th-percentile frame time on a recordWithMedia-heavy scroll session returns under the 8.33 ms 120 Hz budget on a Pixel 10 Pro XL release build, OR a documented decision that the current cost is intrinsic to the layout and the budget should be relaxed for this specific embed shape.
- Macrobench scenario (`nubecita-ppj`) gains a recordWithMedia-weighted variant once that module lands.

## Non-goals

- Backporting layer optimizations to 6vq's PostCardQuotedPost (its 95th percentile is already 7 ms — within budget).
- Changing the visual layout (media-above-quote stays per the design).
notes:
## Diagnostic data — `dumpsys gfxinfo framestats` (Pixel 10 Pro XL release build)

Captured per-frame Compose phase timings on a 120-frame `recordWithMedia`-focused scroll session. Methodology:

```bash
adb shell dumpsys gfxinfo net.kikin.nubecita reset    # clear cumulative stats
# scroll for ~5s on recordWithMedia-heavy content (news/journalism feeds)
adb shell dumpsys gfxinfo net.kikin.nubecita framestats > umn-framestats.txt
# parse the ---PROFILEDATA--- CSV section: subtract consecutive timestamps
# (IntendedVsync → HandleInputStart → AnimationStart → PerformTraversalsStart →
#  DrawStart → SyncQueued → SyncStart → IssueDrawCommandsStart → SwapBuffers →
#  FrameCompleted) to get per-phase deltas in nanoseconds.
```

### Per-phase percentile breakdown (n=120, all non-skipped)

| Phase | p50 | p90 | p95 | p99 | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| input | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | UI-thread input handling |
| animation | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | Compose animation tick |
| measure_layout | 0.06 | 0.08 | 0.08 | 0.15 | Compose layout pass on UI thread |
| draw | 0.47 | 0.62 | 0.67 | 1.07 | UI thread building the DisplayList |
| sync | 0.22 | 0.26 | 0.28 | 0.36 | UI → Render-thread handoff |
| **issue_draw** | **4.19** | **4.98** | **5.60** | **6.24** | **Render thread issuing GPU draw commands** |
| swap | 1.19 | 1.46 | 1.73 | 2.50 | GPU buffer swap |

(times in milliseconds)

### Over-budget attribution

35 of 120 frames (29% in the recordWithMedia-focused sample) exceeded the 8.33 ms 120 Hz budget. **100% of those over-budget frames have `issue_draw` as the dominant phase.** Every single over-budget frame is bottlenecked on render-thread GPU command issuance. No exceptions.

Worst 5 frames in the sample (total / per-phase):

| Total | layout | draw | sync | issue_draw | swap |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 12.7 ms | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.2 | **4.1** | 1.0 |
| 12.4 ms | 0.2 | 1.9 | 0.3 | **5.4** | 1.4 |
| 11.6 ms | 0.1 | 0.6 | 0.2 | **7.9** | 1.1 |
| 10.4 ms | 0.1 | 0.7 | 0.2 | **5.6** | 1.3 |
| 10.2 ms | 0.1 | 0.6 | 0.2 | **6.2** | 1.4 |

### What this rules out

- **Recomposition storms** — UI-thread phases (input/animation/measure/draw) are all <1 ms in steady-state. The Compose Compiler stability report (everything `restartable skippable`) is corroborated at runtime.
- **Layout pass complexity** — `measure_layout` median is 0.06 ms. The marker-interface hierarchy change to `EmbedUi` is not bloating layout work.
- **DisplayList construction (UI-thread Draw)** — 0.47 ms median; 1.95 ms max. Not the bottleneck.
- **Image loading thrash** — long-tail image-fetch frames show up as 36–117 ms outliers in cumulative `gfxinfo`, not in this 120-frame sample. Steady-state regression is unrelated.
- **ExoPlayer / Media3** — `issue_draw` is the bottleneck for non-video frames in this sample too. Video sub-shape may be hottest but isn't the underlying cause; the layer count is.
- **Sub-rect visibility / `onGloballyPositioned` plumbing** — irrelevant; the issue is rendering, not visibility resolution.

### What this confirms

The architectural hypothesis is correct. `PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed` stacks two leaf composables (media + `PostCardQuotedPost` Surface) inside a `Column`. Each leaf allocates its own RenderNode. The render thread spends extra time issuing `drawRenderNode` commands for the additional layer when these posts are visible.

### Refined investigation order (replaces the original "investigation directions")

1. **Layout Inspector layer-count comparison** — Open the app on a recordWithMedia post and a plain quote post side-by-side; compare RenderNode counts. Should show ~2× for recordWithMedia.
2. **`Modifier.graphicsLayer` at `PostCardRecordWithMediaEmbed`'s root** — hoists the children into a single offscreen layer. Re-capture framestats; expect `issue_draw` p95 to drop ~50% (from 5.6 ms to ~2.8 ms), pushing total p95 back under the budget.
3. **If `graphicsLayer` doesn't fully recover** — try restructuring as a single `Layout` with a custom `MeasurePolicy` + `Canvas`-style draw-to-the-RenderNode-directly approach. Higher dev cost; only if (2) is insufficient.
4. **Verify with the official client** — `dumpsys gfxinfo framestats` on Bluesky's own Android app on the same recordWithMedia content. If their numbers are similar, the cost is intrinsic to the layout and the budget should be relaxed for this shape rather than further engineered.

### Recovery headroom

Max `issue_draw` in the sample is 7.95 ms. Even 50% reduction via `graphicsLayer` hoisting recovers ~4 ms — comfortably enough to put 95th-percentile total back under the 8.33 ms budget (current 95th = 10.15 ms; target = ≤8.33 ms; required reduction = ~1.8 ms).

### Acceptance (refined)

- 95th-percentile total frame time on a recordWithMedia-heavy scroll session returns under the 8.33 ms 120 Hz budget on a Pixel 10 Pro XL release build (`-PdebugSignedRelease=true`).
- `issue_draw` p95 drops from 5.60 ms to ≤3 ms.
- OR a documented decision (with comparison data from the official Bluesky Android client) that the cost is intrinsic to the visual layout and the budget should be relaxed for this specific embed shape.

title: Feed: ReasonPin header (📌 Pinned post)
description:
## Why

`FeedViewPost.reason` is currently dispatched only on `ReasonRepost` (we render the "Alice reposted" header). The other concrete reason type — `ReasonPin` — is silently dropped: pinned posts on a profile feed render exactly like any other post, with no indication they're pinned.

This is mostly visible on profile feeds (`app.bsky.feed.getAuthorFeed`), not the home timeline (`getTimeline`). Still worth implementing because the dispatch site is the same one we just touched in nubecita-sbc.4.

Surfaced during review of nubecita-sbc.4 (PR #59); user spotted it as a coverage gap.

## Tasks

- Extend `PostUi` (in `:data:models`) with an `isPinned: Boolean = false` field (or equivalent — design as part of this issue).
- Extend `feature/feed/impl/.../data/FeedViewPostMapper.kt` to set `isPinned = true` when `reason is ReasonPin`. The existing `ReasonRepost` arm stays as-is.
- Add a `PinnedLine` composable to `:designsystem/component/PostCard.kt` that mirrors `RepostedByLine`: a small pin icon (`Icons.Outlined.PushPin` or similar) + "Pinned post" label, sits above the avatar row. Localizable string `R.string.postcard_pinned_post`.
- Mapper unit test: fixture-JSON timeline entry with `reason: app.bsky.feed.defs#reasonPin` → mapper sets `isPinned = true`.
- PostCard preview + screenshot test for the pinned variant.

## Acceptance

- A pinned post renders the "📌 Pinned post" header above the author row.
- A non-pinned post renders unchanged.
- Mapper unit test covers ReasonPin dispatch.
- Screenshot baseline added for the pinned variant.
- Reposted + pinned cannot co-occur on the wire (the bsky reason is a single open union member), so no combined "Pinned + reposted" rendering is needed.

## Out of scope

- The pin/unpin user gesture (creating a pin via post controls). Read-side only.
- Profile-feed scaffolding (`getAuthorFeed`) — that's a separate feature; this issue just makes sure when a pinned post DOES appear in any feed surface, it renders correctly.

title: Feed: reply-context header (Replying to @handle)
description:
## Why

`FeedViewPost.reply` carries the parent + root + grandparent-author context for any reply rendered inside the timeline. Bluesky's first-party clients render a small "Replying to @bob.bsky.social" line above the body so users understand the post is a reply rather than a standalone thought. We currently drop the entire `reply` field on the floor — replies render with no context, which is misleading for users following a thread.

Surfaced during review of nubecita-sbc.4 (PR #59); user spotted it as "I think I see repost/replies and links" in unimplemented coverage.

## Tasks

- Extend `PostUi` (in `:data:models`) with a `replyToHandle: String?` field (or similar — design as part of this issue). Null when the post is not a reply; non-null with the parent author's handle when it is.
- Extend `feature/feed/impl/.../data/FeedViewPostMapper.kt` to populate `replyToHandle` from `FeedViewPost.reply?.parent?.author?.handle?.raw`. Handle the case where parent is a `BlockedPost` / `NotFoundPost` (the lexicon's reply union has multiple variants) — fall through to null in those cases for v1 (no "Replying to a blocked post" UI yet).
- Add a `ReplyToLine` composable to `:designsystem/component/PostCard.kt` that mirrors the existing `RepostedByLine`'s shape: small icon + label, sits above the avatar row, padded to align with the body column. Localizable string `R.string.postcard_reply_to` for "Replying to %1$s".
- Mapper unit test: fixture-JSON timeline entry with `reply.parent` populated → mapper extracts the handle.
- PostCard preview + screenshot test for the reply-context variant.

## Acceptance

- A timeline entry that's a reply renders the "Replying to @bob.bsky.social" line above the author row.
- A standalone post (no `reply` on the wire) renders unchanged.
- Mapper unit tests cover the populated-parent and missing-parent paths.
- Screenshot baselines updated; no FeedScreen baseline regressions outside the deliberate variant.
- `openspec` change should be filed if the design touches the canonical `data-models` or `feature-feed` specs (likely yes — `PostUi` gains a field and `PostCard` gains a render arm).

## Out of scope

- Rendering the parent post itself inline (Bluesky's iOS client sometimes does this — separate, more involved feature).
- Threadgate / postgate awareness.
- Self-thread connector line at the avatar gutter (tracked separately as `nubecita-m28.2`).
close_reason: Superseded by m28.3 / PR #80 — cluster rendering subsumes the single-line 'Replying to @handle' header

title: CI: APK/AAB size-delta PR comment via diffuse-action
description:
## Why

When phase A of `nubecita-sbc` (Media3 deps; PR #57) landed, the only way to know the release `.aab` grew by ~2.1 MB and the debug `.apk` by ~6.5 MB was a manual `git stash` → baseline build → `git stash pop` → re-build → `stat -f%z` diff. That doesn't scale: future Media3-sized deps (or accidental ones) will land without anyone noticing until a release-time review. We need the size delta automated as a PR comment on every PR so the regression catches itself.

## Approach

Adopt [`usefulness/diffuse-action`](https://github.com/usefulness/diffuse-action) — the maintained GitHub Action wrapper around JakeWharton/diffuse (Square's reference APK/AAB diff CLI). The action itself only diffs two artifacts you provide; it does not build anything. The workflow around it builds the PR's artifacts, sources the base artifact (see Caching strategy below), invokes diffuse, and posts a sticky PR comment with total bytes (compressed + uncompressed), per-DEX class counts, resources, native libs, and manifest diff.

Rationale (full shopping-list context in conversation that produced this issue):
- diffuse + diffuse-action are free, OSS, actively maintained (action last released v0.11.0 Jun 2024; last commit Feb 2026; CLI master pushed Apr 2026). Used by Stripe, Signal, Quran-android.
- Lower setup cost than the SaaS alternatives (Emerge Tools, Bitrise) and no third-party data sharing.
- Covers both AAB and APK; matches our `:app:bundleRelease` + `:app:assembleDebug` outputs cleanly.
- Emerge Tools is the richer alternative (per-module / per-class drill-down, historical dashboard) but is a separate scoping question — file a follow-up issue if diffuse's flat output proves insufficient once we have a real release surface.

## Caching strategy

**Critical**: the naive workflow shape is "checkout PR, build, checkout base, build, diff" — that's TWO full `:app:bundleRelease` runs per PR. On our project that's ~5–8 min cold-cache each on a GH Actions Ubuntu runner = ~10–15 min added per PR. Don't ship the naive shape.

**Recommended shape:**
1. **Separate `push` workflow on `main`** builds `:app:bundleRelease` (and optionally `:app:assembleDebug`) and uploads the artifact via `actions/upload-artifact` (or pushes to a cache key keyed on `main`'s SHA — both work; artifact is simpler).
2. **PR workflow** builds only the PR branch's artifacts (1 release build, ~5–8 min cold) and downloads the cached base artifact via `actions/download-artifact` / `dawidd6/action-download-artifact` (~10 sec). Net cost per PR ≈ same as one regular CI build.
3. **Path filter** — skip the size-diff workflow entirely on PRs that don't touch `:app`'s closure. `paths-ignore: ['docs/**', '.github/workflows/**' (except this one), 'openspec/**', '.beads/**', '*.md']` covers the obvious noise.
4. **Optional cost reducer**: diff only the release `.aab` and skip the debug `.apk`. Release is what we actually care about for "did we just ship 2 MB nobody noticed?"; debug delta is mostly compiler / debug-symbol noise.
5. **Stale-cache fallback**: if the base-artifact download fails (e.g., main hasn't been built yet, or cache eviction), fall back to building the base in the PR workflow. Don't fail the workflow — log a warning and continue with the slow path.

A future Gradle remote build cache (separate concern) would make even the slow-path base build cheap, but don't gate this issue on it.

## Tasks

- New workflow file on `main` push: `.github/workflows/main-size-baseline.yml` — builds `:app:bundleRelease`, uploads the `.aab` as a workflow artifact named `app-release-aab-<sha>` with retention ~14 days.
- New workflow file on `pull_request`: `.github/workflows/apk-size-diff.yml` — `paths-ignore` filter for non-code paths; builds PR's `.aab`; downloads cached base `.aab` (with stale-cache fallback to build base in-place); invokes `usefulness/diffuse-action`; the action posts the sticky PR comment.
- Verify diffuse-action's sticky-comment behavior actually replaces (doesn't append) on force-push.
- Document the workflow + caching design in a short ADR / README under `.github/workflows/README.md` so the next person can debug a stale-cache miss without re-deriving the model.

## Acceptance

- Open a no-op PR (e.g., a one-line README edit). The `paths-ignore` filter skips the size-diff workflow entirely (or it runs and posts "no change") within ~5 min — NOT 15 min.
- Open a PR that adds a measurable dependency (e.g., a tiny `implementation` line on a new library). The comment shows the byte delta + DEX-class delta.
- Force-pushing the same PR REPLACES the comment, doesn't accumulate duplicates.
- The first PR after this workflow lands successfully sources the base artifact from the `main` baseline workflow (no stale-cache fallback).
- Workflow is green on `main` after the workflow file lands (i.e., the workflow doesn't crash on its own merge commit).

## Out of scope

- Emerge Tools / SaaS evaluation (separate issue if/when diffuse's output isn't enough).
- Per-feature-module size dashboards (would want `grab/app-sizer` or Emerge for that).
- Historical size tracking dashboard.
- Enforcement / blocking on size growth above a threshold (advisory comment only for v1).
- Gradle remote build cache (separate, larger concern).

## Pre-flight

- Confirm `diffuse-action`'s GitHub-hosted runner can build our project (we use AGP 9.2.0 + JDK 17 via Foojay; first run may need `actions/setup-java` + a JDK 17 distribution).
- Decide artifact-vs-cache for the base: artifact is simpler + has a clean retention model; cache has eviction risk. Lean: artifact.

title: Aggregate Jacoco coverage across all Gradle modules
description:
## Why

PR coverage comments from `madrapps/jacoco-report` consistently say "There is no coverage information present for the Files changed" (visible on PRs #41, #42, and #43) because the `jacocoTestReport` task in `app/build.gradle.kts` is scoped to `:app` only:

- `dependsOn("testDebugUnitTest")` — just `:app` tests
- `classDirectories` from `:app/build/tmp/kotlin-classes/debug`
- `executionData` globbed from `:app/build/...`

Most of the codebase lives outside `:app` (`:core:auth`, `:core:common`, `:core:testing`, `:data:models`, `:designsystem`, `:feature:login:impl`, `:feature:feed:api`, `:feature:feed:impl`). Their tests run, their coverage is generated locally, but the aggregated XML the CI action consumes never sees them — so PRs touching those modules show as 0% / "no coverage."

## What needs to happen

Pick one of:

1. **Gradle `jacoco-report-aggregation` plugin** (modern, native) — apply at root, declare module deps as `jacocoAggregation`, single XML at root build dir. Pass that single path to `madrapps/jacoco-report`.

2. **Per-module reports via convention plugin** (Now in Android pattern) — add jacoco wiring to `AndroidLibraryConventionPlugin` so every module emits its own `jacocoTestReport.xml` under `<module>/build/reports/jacoco/...`; pass all XMLs to the action via comma-separated `paths:`.

3. **Quick patch** — extend the existing `:app:jacocoTestReport` to glob `executionData` and `classDirectories` from sibling modules' build dirs. Smallest change but fragile.

Recommend option 2 — it scales as new modules land and matches the existing convention-plugin pattern in `build-logic/`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] CI's coverage comment on a PR touching `:core:*` or `:feature:*` reports a real percentage (not "no coverage information present").
- [ ] `./gradlew jacocoTestReport` (or equivalent root-level task) runs across all modules.
- [ ] No coverage regression in `:app` (the existing report still works).
- [ ] `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` updated to point at the right XML path(s).

## Notes

- `min-coverage-overall` and `min-coverage-changed-files` are currently 0 in `ci.yaml` — leave at 0 until the aggregated baseline is known. Tightening them is a separate decision.
- Screenshot tests don't produce jacoco data (they run on the AGP screenshot runner, not the unit-test source set), so aggregation is unit-test only.
close_reason: Merged via PR #86 (commit 64a2614)

title: Memoize Brush.linearGradient inside Modifier.shimmer to avoid per-frame allocation
description:
Modifier.shimmer's drawWithCache.onDrawBehind currently rebuilds Brush.linearGradient(colors, start, end) on every frame, since start/end are derived from the animating translate value. Brush is a small object but a 60-120hz animation on every shimmered shape adds up — for a feed full of skeletons that's hundreds of brush allocations per second.

Two paths:
  (a) Cache the brush in drawWithCache itself (the cache callback IS already meant for this) — but the brush depends on translate, which changes every frame. Solution: cache the colors list (which is theme-invariant within a recomposition) and only allocate Brush.linearGradient when start/end change.
  (b) Use a custom Shader / Paint via DrawScope.drawIntoCanvas, mutating the shader's transform per frame instead of rebuilding the brush. Lower-level but allocation-free.

Probably (a) — Compose's Brush class is intended to be cheap to construct, but eliminating the  allocation on the hot path is still a win.

Surfaced during the compose-audit pass on PR #42. Profile-driven optimization — only worth it if Macrobenchmark shows shimmer GC pressure at 120Hz on a populated feed.
acceptance_criteria: Macrobenchmark or Perfetto trace shows Modifier.shimmer's per-frame allocation profile reduced. Visual output unchanged from baseline screenshots.

title: Share a single InfiniteTransition across PostCardShimmer placeholders
description:
Each call to Modifier.shimmer() creates its own rememberInfiniteTransition. PostCardShimmer applies the modifier to ~7 shapes (avatar, 3 text bars, optional image rect, 4 action dots), and a feed full of skeletons multiplies that. Result: every shape has an independent shimmer animation that drifts out of sync over time, so a skeleton card looks like a bag of unrelated shimmers rather than one cohesive surface.

Fix shape: lift the InfiniteTransition + animateFloat above the shapes. Either:
  (a) PostCardShimmer creates the transition once and passes a State<Float> to a sibling Modifier.shimmerWithProgress(progress: Float) that paints the brush from the supplied value (no internal animation).
  (b) Provide a CompositionLocal LocalShimmerProgress that Modifier.shimmer() reads when present and falls back to its own rememberInfiniteTransition when not.

(b) keeps the existing one-call API ergonomic for ad-hoc shimmer use; (a) is more explicit. Pick at impl time.

Surfaced during the compose-audit pass on PR #42 (add-postcard-component change). Not perf-critical — the animations themselves are GPU-cheap — purely a visual cohesion polish.
acceptance_criteria: A LazyColumn of 5+ PostCardShimmer instances renders with all internal shapes (and ideally all cards) shimmering at the same brush translate offset. Verifiable by eye in the existing PostCardShimmer screenshot tests (or via a new perceptual-diff check).

title: PostCard recordWithMedia embed (app.bsky.embed.recordWithMedia)
description:
Render Bluesky recordWithMedia embeds: a composition of #record (quoted post) + media (#images or #video). The lexicon's shape is { record: <recordEmbed>, media: <imagesEmbed | videoEmbed> }.

UX shape: media renders above or below the quoted post card. Most clients render media above the quote (matches the visual hierarchy of 'here's an image, and here's the post I'm quoting').

Scope decision context: filed under nubecita-6rh as v3 — explicitly blocked on its constituent parts being supported. Cannot ship before BOTH the record (v2) AND images-or-video tiers exist.

Implementation notes:
- Mapper sealed type adds RecordWithMedia(record: Record, media: Embed) variant — note Embed is the same sealed parent so this composes naturally.
- Renderer is just a Column/Row composition of the existing embed renderers; little new code if the Record + Images/Video ones are well-factored.
- Edge case: media is .video on a recordWithMedia — defer until the standalone video tier ships.

Decision doc: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-25-postcard-embed-scope-v1.md
acceptance_criteria: RecordWithMedia(record, media) variant added to embed sealed type; composes existing PostCardRecordEmbed + (PostCardImageEmbed | PostCardVideoEmbed); manual smoke on a real recordWithMedia post via Bluesky API.
close_reason: Shipped via PR #64 (squash-merged as e948c23). Closes the v1.x embed-scope roadmap — all current app.bsky.embed.* lexicon variants now have real renderers. 95th-percentile scroll regression on recordWithMedia-heavy content (10ms vs the 8.33ms 120Hz budget) attributed to the render-thread issue_draw phase via gfxinfo framestats analysis; tracked as nubecita-7zl with concrete attribution data + targeted Modifier.graphicsLayer-hoist investigation directions. OpenSpec change at openspec/changes/add-feature-feed-record-with-media-embed/ pending archive in a follow-up.

title: PostCard video embed (app.bsky.embed.video)
description:
Render Bluesky video clip embeds in PostCard. Schema (per app.bsky.embed.video lexicon): a single video clip with an .m3u8 HLS playlist URL, alt text, aspect ratio, and a thumbnail blob.

UX shape: video tile (thumbnail-then-play) with tap-to-play inline OR tap-to-open-in-detail. Inline play needs scroll-aware lifecycle (pause when scrolled offscreen, release ExoPlayer when far from viewport).

Scope decision context: filed under nubecita-6rh as v3 — lands after PostCard v1 (text+images), v1.1 (external), and v2 (record). Multi-day work due to Media3/ExoPlayer integration. Block until a video-embed test post in our feed is identifiable as a real UX gap, not a long-tail rarity.

Open decision (filed separately if needed): Media3 (androidx.media3:media3-exoplayer + media3-ui) vs raw ExoPlayer — Media3 is current and recommended; raw ExoPlayer is deprecated path. Default to Media3 unless something in atproto-kotlin or our feed pipeline forces otherwise.

Implementation notes:
- Mapper sealed type adds Video(playlistUrl, thumbUrl, aspectRatio, altText) variant.
- ExoPlayer instance lifecycle is the hard problem — best practice is one player per visible item with LazyListState awareness. AndroidView{ PlayerView(...) } for the surface.
- HLS playlist (.m3u8) requires media3-exoplayer-hls module.
- Audio: muted by default (matches Bluesky web client behavior); tap to unmute.

Decision doc: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-25-postcard-embed-scope-v1.md
acceptance_criteria: Video(...) variant added to embed sealed type; Media3 ExoPlayer integration ships in :designsystem (or :feature:feed:impl — decide); inline thumbnail-then-play pattern with scroll-aware pause/release; muted-by-default; manual smoke on a real video post via Bluesky API.
close_reason: superseded by nubecita-sbc.4 (PR #59) — video embed work shipped via the sbc epic with full autoplay coordinator

title: Relative-time formatter for post / profile timestamps
description:
Posts, profile join dates, and (later) notifications display times relative to now: "now", "2m", "4h", "5d", then absolute date ("Mar 15") past some threshold (typically a week).

Need:
- A pure helper: (now: Instant, then: Instant) -> String. Deterministic, easily unit-testable.
- A Composable wrapper that recomposes periodically while visible so a "now" post becomes "1m" without scroll. Use a slow ticker (e.g. flow that emits every 30s) consumed via collectAsStateWithLifecycle, or rememberCoroutineScope + delay loop.
- AT Protocol records carry RFC 3339 strings (createdAt). Helper accepts kotlinx.datetime.Instant; the DTO->UI mapper handles the parse.

Lives in :core:common (cross-cutting; not Compose-specific in the core helper, only the auto-recomposing wrapper is) so non-feed surfaces (search results, notifications) can reuse.

Blocks PostCard (nubecita-w0d) — every post displays a timestamp.
acceptance_criteria: Pure formatter helper unit-tested against fixed (now, then) pairs covering second/minute/hour/day/week/absolute-date thresholds; Composable wrapper updates without scroll-triggered recomposition of the whole list; helper uses kotlinx-datetime.
close_reason: Merged in PR #37 (commit cb589f0). Pure helper formatRelativeTime + Composable rememberRelativeTimeText shipped in :core:common with 18 unit tests. Localized via Android string + plural resources (RelativeTimeStrings.English fixture for tests). i18n + lint + Copilot review + compose-audit feedback all addressed in follow-up commits before merge.

title: perf(designsystem): profile thread connector + fold under lazy-list load (3 candidate optimizations)
description:
## Why

PR #77 (`nubecita-m28.2`) landed `Modifier.threadConnector` + `ThreadFold` design-system primitives. The Compose audit pass against the `android-compose-ui` + `compose-performance-audit` skills found no current performance issues — these components do their work in the draw phase, use stable parameters, and avoid `composed { }`. But three speculative optimizations are worth keeping on the books in case profiling under high-volume lazy-list usage (long feeds, scrolling at 120Hz with the cross-author cluster in `nubecita-m28.3` actively rendering) reveals them as hotspots.

**Do not start this ticket without profiling evidence first.** All three items are optimizations against synthetic concerns, not measured regressions. Premature implementation adds complexity without payoff. Macrobenchmark a feed scroll under realistic conditions and act on the data — if `:designsystem`'s thread components don't show up in the trace, close this ticket as won't-fix.

## Items

### 1. Caller-side lambda memoization for `ThreadFold.onClick`

`ThreadFold(onClick = ...)` accepts an unstable `() -> Unit` lambda. ThreadFold itself stays composition-stable across lambda churn, but a caller in a `LazyColumn` item that writes:

```kotlin
ThreadFold(onClick = { navigator.add(PostDetail(post.uri)) })
```

allocates a fresh lambda per recomposition of the item. At 120Hz with a long feed of clusters, this could pressure the allocator.

**Fix pattern (caller side, not ThreadFold's concern):**

```kotlin
val onFoldClick = remember(post.uri) { { navigator.add(PostDetail(post.uri)) } }
ThreadFold(onClick = onFoldClick)
```

Where to apply: every callsite of `ThreadFold` and `Modifier.threadConnector` inside `LazyColumn`/`LazyRow` items. Initial known callsite: the cross-author cluster from `nubecita-m28.3`.

**Trigger:** profile a feed scroll with a m28.3 cluster present. If allocations attributable to lambdas in the cluster show up in the heap profiler, apply this pattern. Otherwise leave alone — `remember` itself has overhead and shouldn't be deployed defensively.

### 2. Custom `Modifier.Node` for `threadConnector`

The current `Modifier.drawWithContent { ... }` factory allocates a new `DrawWithContentModifier` and a new lambda on every call to `Modifier.threadConnector(...)`. For a feed with N self-thread chains and M cluster posts, that's `2 * (N * chainLength + M * 3)` allocations per recomposition pass.

A bespoke `Modifier.Node` implementation would let the modifier keep its parameters in fields on the Node instance and only recreate the Node when params change — eliminating the per-call lambda allocation entirely.

**Sketch:**

```kotlin
private data class ThreadConnectorElement(
    val connectAbove: Boolean,
    val connectBelow: Boolean,
    val color: Color,
    val gutterX: Dp,
    val avatarTop: Dp,
    val avatarBottom: Dp,
    val strokeWidth: Dp,
) : ModifierNodeElement<ThreadConnectorNode>() {
    override fun create() = ThreadConnectorNode(this)
    override fun update(node: ThreadConnectorNode) { node.update(this) }
}

private class ThreadConnectorNode(private var spec: ThreadConnectorElement) :
    Modifier.Node(), DrawModifierNode {
    fun update(spec: ThreadConnectorElement) { this.spec = spec; invalidateDraw() }
    override fun ContentDrawScope.draw() {
        drawContent()
        if (!spec.connectAbove && !spec.connectBelow) return
        // ... same drawLine logic, reading spec.* ...
    }
}

fun Modifier.threadConnector(...): Modifier =
    this then ThreadConnectorElement(...)
```

**Trigger:** Macrobenchmark on a feed with 50+ posts where ~30% are cluster members. If `Modifier.threadConnector` shows non-trivial GC pressure or recomposition allocation in `Allocation Tracker`, do the rewrite. Otherwise skip — Node-based modifiers are more code, harder to read, and only pay off at scale.

### 3. `drawWithContent` → `drawBehind` swap

`Modifier.drawWithContent` lets you control draw order via explicit `drawContent()` call. `Modifier.drawBehind` always draws BEFORE content with no `drawContent()` management.

The connector lines live in the avatar gutter (x=42dp) where the post body normally has no content (post body starts at x=76dp), so visually `drawBehind` and `drawWithContent { drawContent(); ... }` produce identical output. `drawBehind` is marginally simpler and avoids one stack-frame's worth of work per draw.

**Why we kept `drawWithContent`:** forward-compat. If a future post layout draws in the gutter (e.g., an attribution sticker, a verified badge spilling into the gutter, an animated avatar ring), `drawWithContent` lets us decide whether the connector goes above or below it. With `drawBehind`, the connector is always behind, period.

**Trigger:** if profiling shows `drawWithContent` overhead in the trace AND the team is confident no future gutter-painting post variants are coming, swap to `drawBehind`. The savings are sub-microsecond per draw call. Realistically: never, unless `Modifier.drawWithContent` is deprecated in some future Compose version.

## Acceptance

- Macrobenchmark trace of a feed scroll with thread clusters present is captured + linked in the ticket notes.
- For each of the three items, either:
  - The trace shows the issue is real → implement and verify post-fix trace shows improvement, OR
  - The trace shows the issue isn't real → close the relevant item with a one-line "profiled, not a hotspot" note.
- No item is implemented without supporting trace data.

## References

- Compose audit pass on PR #77 (in commit f6f34c0's discussion / chat log) — found no current issues; these are forward-looking only.
- `:designsystem/component/ThreadConnector.kt` — the modifier factory.
- `:designsystem/component/ThreadFold.kt` — the composable.
- Future consumer: `nubecita-m28.3` (cross-author reply clusters in feed).

## Non-goals

- General-purpose `:designsystem` perf audit — this ticket is scoped specifically to the thread components.
- Implementing any of the three items without profiling evidence — see "Why."

title: feat(app): production release signing via Play App Signing
description:
## Why

Following nubecita-e7x, App Distribution to internal testers ships **debug-signed** APKs. Distributing to Play Store requires release-signed builds. The chosen approach is Google's **Play App Signing**: Google holds the app signing key (the one users' devices ultimately validate); we manage only an **upload key**. Lower blast radius than self-managed release signing — losing the upload key is recoverable via a Play Console rotation; losing a self-managed app signing key bricks the app.

This ticket sets up the upload-key plumbing so CI can build, sign, and upload `assembleRelease` / `bundleRelease` artifacts to Play Store, completing the production distribution path.

## Scope

### Play Console setup
- Enroll the app in Play App Signing (Play Console → app → Setup → App signing).
- Google generates the app signing key and holds it server-side; we generate the upload key locally.

### Upload key generation + registration
- Generate `nubecita-upload.keystore` locally (NOT committed). Cap key validity per Play Console requirements (≥25 years).
- Register the upload key's SHA-1 + SHA-256 in Firebase console → `net.kikin.nubecita` (for App Check Play Integrity validation of release builds).
- Register the upload key in Play Console → app signing.

### CI wiring
- Encode upload keystore as base64. Add GitHub repository secrets:
  - `RELEASE_KEYSTORE_BASE64`
  - `RELEASE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`
  - `RELEASE_KEY_ALIAS`
  - `RELEASE_KEY_PASSWORD`
- In `:app/build.gradle.kts`: configure `signingConfigs.release` reading from env-provided keystore path. Wire `buildTypes.release.signingConfig`. Keep `isMinifyEnabled = true` and `proguardFiles` (this is also where R8 / shrink rules will need to land — out of scope for this ticket but note as adjacent).
- New CI workflow (or extend `release.yaml`): build `bundleRelease` (AAB), sign with upload key, upload to Play Store via `r0adkll/upload-google-play-action` or equivalent. Service account scoped to Play Console (separate from the Firebase service account used for App Distribution).

### Distribution path
- Production users get the app via Play Store (Google re-signs with the app signing key automatically before distributing to devices).
- Internal testers continue receiving debug builds via Firebase App Distribution (the e7x path is unchanged).

## Acceptance

- App is enrolled in Play App Signing.
- `nubecita-upload.keystore` is generated, registered in Firebase + Play Console, and stored as a GitHub secret.
- CI builds and uploads a release-signed AAB to Play Store on a tag/release event.
- The released app on Play Store passes App Check Play Integrity validation (production telemetry shows valid tokens, no rejections of legitimate traffic).
- Release-signed AAB is reproducible from a clean checkout given the four `RELEASE_*` secrets and the upload keystore.

## Non-goals

- Migration off App Distribution for internal testing. We keep both paths: App Distribution for debug builds, Play Store for production.
- Track promotion automation (alpha → beta → production). Initial setup uploads to internal track only; promotion is manual until a separate ticket addresses it.
- R8 / minification tuning beyond the defaults — adjacent concern, separate ticket if shrink/optimize behavior surprises us.
- Crashlytics, Performance Monitoring, Remote Config — separate tickets per nubecita-e7x non-goals.

## References

- Originating ticket: nubecita-e7x — Firebase integration.
- Play App Signing: https://developer.android.com/studio/publish/app-signing#app-signing-google-play
- Firebase App Check Play Integrity: https://firebase.google.com/docs/app-check/android/play-integrity-provider

title: Replace .weight(1f).fillMaxSize() with .weight(1f).fillMaxHeight() in PostCardImageEmbed
description:
PostCardImageEmbed's TwoImages/ThreeImages/FourImages helpers chain Modifier.weight(1f).fillMaxSize() on Row children. weight(1f) already takes the available space along the parent's flex axis (width for Row, height for Column), so fillMaxSize() is only adding the cross-axis fill — which fillMaxHeight() (or fillMaxWidth() for Column children) expresses more precisely.

Pure semantic readability cleanup. No behavioral change — the rendered layout is identical.

Surfaced during the compose-audit pass on PR #42. Backlog quality work; pick up alongside any other PostCardImageEmbed change.
acceptance_criteria: Modifier chains in PostCardImageEmbed use .weight(1f).fillMaxHeight() (Row children) or .weight(1f).fillMaxWidth() (Column children). Existing screenshot baselines unchanged.

value:
When starting a remote/away-from-home Claude session where the 1Password SSH agent can't sign (requires fingerprint touch on the user's Mac), disable commit signing locally for this repo:

  git config --local commit.gpgsign false

Re-enable on return by unsetting the local override:

  git config --local --unset commit.gpgsign

Why: global config has commit.gpgsign=true with gpg.format=ssh and gpg.ssh.program=/Applications/1Password.app/Contents/MacOS/op-ssh-sign. On remote sessions every commit fails with 'sign_and_send_pubkey: signing failed' the same way SSH git ops fail. Disabling locally lets commits land unsigned during the remote session; the global signed-by-default behavior is preserved everywhere else.

This is the commit-side counterpart to the gh-https-fallback memory (which handles fetch/push on the same machines).

When squashing a feature branch via 'git reset --soft origin/main' + recommit, ALWAYS rebase onto current origin/main FIRST. The reset --soft pattern computes diff(working_tree, origin/main) — if main has moved forward with commits your working tree doesn't have (e.g. previous PR in the series merged), those commits show up as DELETIONS in your squash, effectively reverting them on your branch. Symptom: PR diff includes unrelated 'reverts' of recently-merged code. Safe sequence: (1) git fetch origin, (2) git rebase origin/main (resolves any conflicts), (3) make any new edits on top, (4) reset --soft origin/main, (5) recommit. For proposal-only or single-file PRs, an even safer recovery is: git reset --hard origin/main + git checkout <commit> -- <intended-paths> + recommit. Burned during PR #56 (sbc.2 video proposal) which accidentally reverted PR #55 (zk2 a11y) because the reset-soft was done while my working tree was still on the pre-#55-merge state of main.

Auth strategy is OAuth-only. App-password path was dropped on 2026-04-24; nubecita-ym3 closed as superseded by nubecita-ck0. Session storage (nubecita-nss) persists a single OAuthSession as defined by the atproto-oauth library. Epic nubecita-gr4 title still reads 'app password + OAuth' but scope is OAuth-only — retitle when next touched.

When SSH agent can't sign on this machine (1Password SSH agent requires fingerprint touch — fails on remote/away-from-home Claude sessions), bypass SSH for git network operations using gh's HTTPS credential helper:

  git -c credential.helper= -c credential.helper='!gh auth git-credential' fetch https://github.com/kikin81/nubecita.git +refs/heads/main:refs/remotes/origin/main

For push, swap to:
  git -c credential.helper= -c credential.helper='!gh auth git-credential' push https://github.com/kikin81/nubecita.git <branch>

Why: User is often on remote Claude sessions (not home, can't touch 1Password fingerprint). Repo's origin remote URL is git@github.com:kikin81/nubecita.git which won't work in those cases. The gh CLI is logged in via keyring with repo scope, so the credential helper short-circuits SSH entirely. Don't modify the remote URL or ssh config — the credential-helper override on a single command keeps the repo config clean.

How to apply: When 'git fetch' or 'git push' fails with 'sign_and_send_pubkey: signing failed' or 'Permission denied (publickey)', do NOT retry SSH or fiddle with ssh-agent — switch to the gh HTTPS one-liner above. Confirm gh auth is still good with 'gh auth status' first if uncertain.

Prefer OpenSpec for design + spec docs over docs/superpowers/specs/.

Layout:
- In-flight design + tasks → openspec/changes/<change-name>/  (use openspec-propose / opsx:propose)
- Durable capability specs → openspec/specs/<capability>/ (e.g. feature-feed, design-system)
- Archive completed changes via openspec-archive-change / opsx:archive — that step also folds updates into openspec/specs/

Why: keeps all specs in one directory tree under repo governance instead of fragmenting between docs/superpowers/specs/ and openspec/. Makes archival, cross-linking, and reviewer discovery cleaner. The repo already uses OpenSpec for past feed/video work (see openspec/changes/archive and the feature-feed-video spec).

How to apply: when the user (or a bd issue) calls for a design doc or spec, default to opening an OpenSpec change rather than dropping a markdown file in docs/superpowers/specs/. For existing docs in docs/superpowers/specs/, propose migration when the related feature is being archived/closed — don't churn them in isolation.

When syncing a feature branch with main, always rebase (git fetch && git rebase origin/main) — never merge. Merge commits are rejected. After rebase, force-push with --force-with-lease. This applies even when the original sync was started as a merge — collapse via 'git reset --soft origin/main' + single commit + force-push-with-lease, no interactive rebase (the -i flag is not supported by this harness). Squash multi-commit feature branches into one semantic commit before opening or after updating a PR.

Always verify a commit landed BEFORE pushing — check 'git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD' shows the new commit. The pre-commit hook can apply auto-fixes (trailing whitespace, end-of-files, line endings) that leave the staged file with NEW unstaged changes; if the hook returns non-zero or the staging diverges from the index, the commit aborts SILENTLY and only the hook output is printed. The 'tail -3' filter on commit output can hide the missing commit-success line. Then 'git push --force-with-lease' will happily push the unchanged HEAD (whatever that is — possibly origin/main's tip from a prior reset --soft), causing GitHub to auto-close the PR because branch tip == base. Burned during PR #56 (sbc.2 video proposal). Fix: after every commit, run 'git log --oneline -1' to confirm a new SHA, OR pipe with 'tail -10' instead of 'tail -3' to capture the commit confirmation line.
